cards: Imaging of small and large intestine pathology
Investigation and management of the acute airway case\nA 3-year-old presents with a short history of fever, drooling and stridor.

cards.text: Transfer to theatre with experienced anaesthetist and surgeon

cards: Investigations in lymphatic disorders\nThis is best to exclude a pelvic or intra-abdominal mass causing leg swelling.
Ureteric pathology imaging

cards.text: Retrograde pyelogram showing transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis
IVU showing transitional cell carcinoma of the left renal pelvis
Antegrade pyelogram showing transitional cell carcinoma of the renal pelvis

cards: Conditions of the middle ear\nWhich of the following statements regarding middle ear effusion in adults are true?

cards.text: It may be the presenting feature of nasopharyngeal carcinoma
If persistent, it should be treated medically

cards: Phases of wound healing\nThis phase is characterised by replacement of type 3 collagen by type 1 until a ratio of 4:1 is achieved. Realignment of collagen fibres along the lines of tension, decreased vascularity and wound contraction are also seen in this phase.
Causes of red eye\nA 55-year-old female presents with moderately severe pain in the right eye. On examination the temporal and superior sclera is a deep red colour, with some overlying oedema.
Parotid surgery\nWhich of the following branches of the facial nerve can be divided without the need for immediate cable graft repair?
Eyelid lumps\nA 72-year-old female presents with a tense, fluctuant swelling just medial to the medial canthus of the left eye.
Surgical techniques\nWhat is used in incision and curettage of a meibomian cyst?

cards.text: A tarsal conjunctiva incision tangential to the lid margin
A tarsal conjunctiva incision radial to the lid margin
A skin incision tangential to the lid margin
A skin incision radial to the lid margin

cards: Treatment of oropharyngeal cancer\nWhat effect does the presence of cervical metastasis have on prognosis in head and neck cancer?
Investigations in anorectal symptoms\nA 65-year-old male is referred with history of a lump in the anus. Examination reveals a 1.5 cm hard lump at the anal verge.
Epistaxis\nWhat may be involved in the management of life-threatening epistaxis?

cards.text: Ligation of the external carotid artery below the origin of the lingual artery
Ligation of the internal carotid artery.
Ligation of the external carotid artery above the origin of the lingual artery

cards: Lump in the abdomen\nA 45-year-old female complains of pain in the right upper abdomen since the previous night, which is severe in intensity and only partially relieved on taking analgesics. The pain is also radiating to the back on the right side and is associated with nausea and vomiting. She gives a history of intolerance to fatty foods in the past, associated with slight discomfort in the right upper abdomen. On examination there is tenderness on palpation at the tip of the ninth costal cartilage in the midclavicular line on the right side, and a lump is palpable in the same region. The rest of the abdomen is normal.
Infections\nThis produces a well-demarcated infection by streptococci, usually on the face.
Cardiac disease\nOn routine chest radiography, a 78-year-old male is suspected of having an aneurysm of the descending thoracic aorta. What is the appropriate investigation?

cards.text: Computed tomography (CT) scan of the chest

cards: The parotid gland\nWhich of the following structures does not lie in the parotid gland?

cards.text: Terminal branches of the external carotid

cards: Gastric operations\nThis is a non-selective operation used previously for peptic ulcers. Gastric stasis is a common postoperative problem and hence this was combined with a drainage procedure.

Abdominal distension\nA 35-year-old man presents with severe central abdominal colicky pain for the past 8 hours. It is associated with vomiting and abdominal distension. He has also not moved his bowels since the onset of pain. He underwent an appendicectomy as a child. On examination the abdomen has central distension that is resonant on percussion. The abdomen is tense but non- tender. The bowel sounds are exaggerated and high-pitched. Abdominal X-rays show the presence of multiple air-fluid levels distributed mainly in the central abdomen.
Imaging of the nose and paranasal sinuses\nA 16-year-old male presents with unilateral nasal obstruction and intermittent unilateral epistaxis.

cards.text: Culture and sensitivity of nasal mucus
Mucosal biopsy and blood test for ESR and ANCA

cards: Differential diagnosis of upper GI presentations\nA 63-year-old male presents with repeated non-bilious vomiting containing ingested food. He has upper abdominal fullness and has lost 3 stones in weight. He is found to be pale and cachectic.
Fractures, dislocations and subluxations\nA patient has a hyperflexion injury. The X-ray shows that the vertical height of C4 vertebra is 50 per cent less at the front than the back.
Endoscopic diagnosis\nThere is evidence of gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) and a linear tongue of erythematous mucosa extending for 3 cm into the oesophagus above.

Differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis\nA 28-year-old female complains of sudden onset of severe right-sided abdominal pain which he is unable to localise. He is in agony, writhing around and cannot find a comfortable position in which to get any relief from his pain. He has some strangury. On examination he is tender all over the right side of his abdomen with some rigidity but no rebound. On percussion there is a tympanitic note.
Eyelid lumps\nA 2-year-old child presents with acute swelling and redness of the eyelids of the left eye. The underlying eye and orbit appear normal.
Bone tumours\nAn elderly patient is found to have fractured through a lytic lesion in the proximal femur following a minor trip. What is the likely cause of the lesion?
Cerebral physiology\nCerebral autoregulation maintains normal cerebral blood flow in what range of mean arterial blood pressures?

Chest trauma\nA 25-year-old motorcyclist has been brought to the A&E department in a panicky state as he is unable to breathe properly and is intensely hypoxic (oxygen saturation of 90 per cent). The trachea is shifted to the right, the left hemithorax does not move and there is hyperresonance over the left chest wall. The noise in the A&E department makes listening to breath sounds difficult.
Hypertension and hypokalaemia\nA 32-year-old woman presents with resistant hypertension.

cards.text: Urine K + , laxative and diuretic screen

cards: Benign skin tumours\nA benign skin tumour caused by the human papillomavirus (HPV) which also causes plantar warts and condylomata acuminata.
Aetiology of anorectal disorders\nThe cause is usually cryptogenic and associated with sepsis in the terminal part of the anal glands.
Anatomy\nThe posterior border of the oropharynx

cards.text: Junction of anterior 2/3 and posterior 1/3 of the tongue

cards: Methods of holding fractures\nThe woman in Scenario 5 has the fracture reduced but it will not stay in position.
Investigation and management of the acute airway case\nA 28-year-old who came off his motorbike and struck his neck on a lamp post presents with increasing stridor.

Congenital malformations\nA 2-month-old Down’s syndrome baby has been brought in with gradual abdominal distension, intermittent bilious vomiting with a history of delayed passage of meconium. The parents feel that the baby is unduly constipated. Examination shows a baby that has not been thriving normally, with a hugely distended abdomen and gross dehydration.
Postoperative oliguria\nWhich of the following statements with regard to postoperative oliguria are false?

cards.text: Renal US is carried out to look for hydronephrosis from blocked ureters.
Oliguria is defined as <1 mL urine/kg of body weight per h.
Patients undergoing an operation for obstructive jaundice are particularly susceptible.
The commonest cause is inadequate fluid replacement.

cards: Differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis\nA 25-year-old female complains of severe pain in her central lower abdomen of 4 h duration. It started with some initial discomfort around her suprapubic area. She feels faint and is very thirsty. She is not sure about her last menstrual period and is on the oral contraceptive pill (OCP). On examination she is in agony, looks pale and is cold, clammy and sweaty. She is apyrexial and has marked tenderness, rigidity and rebound tenderness over her entire lower abdomen. She has some discoloration around her umbilicus.
Statistical tests\nTo compare two groups which are numerical and normally distributed.

Acute abdomen\nA 10-year-old boy has been brought in with abdominal pain, vomiting, pyrexia and diarrhoea for the last 24 h or so. He has been off his food for a couple of days. He has pyrexia of 39°C, tachycardia, looks toxic and has marked lower abdominal tenderness, rigidity and rebound tenderness, and the abdominal wall does not move with respiration.

Fluid and electrolyte abnormalities\nAn 87-year-old woman is admitted to hospital following a vertebral fracture. Blood tests following operative repair to stabilise the fracture are as follows: Na, 123 mmol/L; K, 3.9 mmol/L; total protein, 102 g/L; albumin, 30 g/L; creatinine 134 μ mol/L.

Sequelae of gastric surgery\nA 58-year-old male who has had previous gastric resection for his peptic ulcer presents with epigastric fullness, sweating, light-headedness and diarrhoea almost immediately after eating. The attack last for half an hour and is relieved by lying down and aggravated by more food.
Conditions of the external ear\nWhere does the external ear develop from?

cards.text: The first and second branchial arches
The second and third branchial arches.

cards: Chest trauma\nFollowing an injury at a building site where a piece of heavy masonry fell on his chest, a 35-year-old man has been sent to the A&E department with severe pain in his chest and marked bruising of the chest wall on the left. On examination his blood pressure is 80/50 mmHg, the left chest is dull on percussion and there is no air entry.
Congenital abnormalities\nIn rupture of the membranous urethra, which of the following statements is false?

cards.text: The prostate may be high-riding and out of reach on rectal examination.
It is usually a part of multiple trauma.
It is almost always associated with a pelvic fracture.
A urethral catheter is inserted as a part of initial resuscitation.
10–15 per cent of fractured pelvis cases will have associated urethral injury.

cards: Clinical presentations of small and large intestine pathology\nA 60-year-old woman complains of feeling tired while doing her usual household work over the last 3 months. She has been short of breath climbing one flight of stairs at home. Her haemoglobin is 8.5 g/L. Abdominal examination reveals a mobile mass in the right iliac fossa.

Hand injury\nThe patient got a thorn in their finger when gardening. Now the finger is red and swollen. Even passive flexion is painful, and pressing on the palmar side of the finger is very sore. Choose and match the correct diagnosis with each of the following scenarios:

cards.text: Disjunction of the palmar arch on the ulnar side
Malunion of an oblique fracture of the proximal phalanx

cards: Case study – sudden onset of urinary incontinence\nA 30-year-old nurse lifting in the ward experiences sudden pain in her back. She goes to Sister’s office to sit down. The pain eases but now she notices that she has difficulty standing and walking (her legs feel weak and wobbly, and she has been incontinent of urine). What is the rare but very dangerous diagnosis which needs excluding?

cards.text: Dissecting aneurysm blocking off the blood supply to the spinal cord.
Collapsed vertebral body secondary to occult myeloma

cards: Otological sepsis\nA 28-year-old with a history of recent viral illness followed by otalgia presents to casualty with a pyrexia and a Glasgow Coma Scale of 9.

cards.text: Urgent contrast-enhanced CT scan of temporal bones and brain
Prescribe analgesia with systemic antibiotics if symptoms do not settle within 48 h

cards: Maxillofacial trauma\nA 60-year-old female suffers a severe injury to the bony orbit on the left side. Radiographic examination suggests medial displacement of the greater wing of the sphenoid, with evidence of reduction in the dimensions of the superior orbital fissure.

cards.text: Increasing proptosis, marked subconjunctival oedema, loss of direct light reflex with preservation of the consensual light reflex
Ptosis of the upper eyelid and forehead paraesthesia

cards: Renal pathology imaging

Elbow injury\nA patient with rheumatoid arthritis attends with pain in the elbow mainly on pronation and supination. For each scenario, choose the test or investigation that is likely to be most useful for diagnosis:
Case studies – shoulder injury\nA 20-year-old man sustains a dislocation of the shoulder playing rugby. What would you tell him is the chance of this shoulder dislocating again?
Management of hip conditions\nA patient of 80 has severe osteoarthritis of the hip, limiting walking and preventing sleep.

Interventions/assessment in the high-risk surgical patient\nThis is invaluable in critically ill patients who require IV fluid replacements or vasoactive infusions to stabilise their circulatory status.
Varicose veins\nWhich of the following regarding varicose veins are true?

cards.text: The prevalence of varicose veins is twice as common in women than in men.
Handheld Doppler provides an accurate assessment of saphenopopliteal competence.
Varicose veins may cause venous claudication.
Right leg varicose veins are more common than left leg varicose veins.
Varicose veins may be associated with the fOXC2 gene.

cards: Shoulder injury\nA young man injures his shoulder playing rugby. It feels as if it came out of place but went back in again. It was swollen and painful for some weeks but is now better. However, when the examiner lifts the arm up and back he becomes very apprehensive and asks them to stop. For each scenario, choose the most appropriate treatment:

cards.text: Advice and commiseration; no active treatment

cards: Types of fracture union\nA young man has a fracture of the tibia in a football accident. It is fixed with a locking intramedullary nail. At review 3 months later, the fracture site is red and hot. There is a lot of pain and no sign of union of the fracture.
Specific infections\nA 16-year-old boy who had an appendicectomy for a gangrenous appendix 1 week ago presents with diarrhoea, fever and lower abdominal pain.

Vomiting\nA 40-year-old male smoker presents with history of projectile non-bilious vomiting for the past week. The vomitus contains residue of food items eaten previously and is foul- smelling. The vomiting is associated with bouts of severe upper abdominal colicky pain that are relieved after vomiting. He gives history of episodes of upper abdominal gnawing pain in the past. On examination, the patient is dehydrated and has a pulse rate of 100/min and a BP of 100/60 mmHg. There is distension in the epigastrium and umbilical regions with presence of a succussion splash on auscultation.

cards.text: Gastric outlet obstruction – chronic duodenal ulcer

cards: Aetiology of anorectal disorders\nThe most common cause is an occult obstetric sphincter injury.
Imaging of the nose and paranasal sinuses\nA 35-year-old female with simple nasal polyps has failed maximal medical therapy and wishes to consider surgical management.

cards.text: Culture and sensitivity of nasal mucus
Mucosal biopsy and blood test for ESR and ANCA

cards: Scrotal swelling\nA 50-year-old male who is a known chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) patient complains of sudden onset of increased breathlessness and easy fatiguability over the past 2 weeks. It is associated with progressive distension of the abdomen over the past 2 weeks and swelling of bilateral scrotum in the last week. On examination, he has bilateral fine crepitations at the lung bases, the jugular venous pressure (JVP) is raised and he is dyspnoeic at rest. There is tender enlargement of the liver with free fluid in the peritoneal cavity. The scrotum is transilluminant and the swelling decreases on elevation of the scrotum.
Diseases of the larynx\nWhich of the following statements regarding laryngectomy is true?

cards.text: The procedure can treat malignancy without affecting the voice
The procedure involves separation of the airway from the GI tract.
It is the recommended treatment for early laryngeal cancer.
The surgical tracheostomy may be closed once postoperative swelling subsides.

cards: Chronic pain control\nWhich of the following is not an alternative strategy for the treatment of chronic pain?
Fractures, dislocations and subluxations\nCT of the patient's cervical spine reveals a transverse fracture through the neck of the odontoid peg.
Lower limb swelling\nA 60-year-old man has marked leg swelling 1 day after undergoing a femoral distal bypass for critical ischaemia. Pulses are present but the calf is tense and tender.

Initial investigations\nA 63-year-old male presents with an acute-onset, complete right third cranial nerve palsy and headache. You are concerned about a possible intracranial aneurysm. What initial investigation(s) would you arrange?

cards.text: Measure blood pressure, random blood glucose and full blood count.
No investigations. Contact the duty ophthalmologist.
Discuss urgent MR angiogram with the duty neuroradiologist.
Arrange urgent CT scan of the orbits.

cards: Elbow injury\nA patient with rheumatoid arthritis attends with pain in the elbow mainly on pronation and supination. For each scenario, choose the treatment most likely to be of benefit in the first instance:

cards.text: Synovectomy and excision of the radial head

cards: Specific infections\nAn immunocompromised patient develops rapidly spreading infection of the abdominal wall after a laparotomy for peritonitis. He complains of severe pain. Examination reveals extensive cellulitis with crepitus. Culture swab reveals mixed aerobic and anaerobic growth.
Investigation and management of a parotid mass\nWhich nerve is it particularly important to test prior to surgery?
Vasopressor and inotropic support in shock\nHypovolaemic shock due to splenic injury.

cards.text: No role for vasopressor or inotropic agent

cards: Acute abdomen\nKerry, a 5-year-old girl, has been brought by her parents with generalised abdominal pain which has been recurrent over 6 months or so. On this occasion she looks ill, out of sorts, anorexic and has been vomiting. She has marked urinary frequency. On examination she has a temperature of 100°C, looks toxic, listless and dehydrated and has generalised abdominal and bilateral loin tenderness.

Vomiting\nA 25-year-old male patient, residing in Sri Lanka, presents with history of off-and-on colicky central abdominal pain over the past 12 months, associated with weight loss that is not documented. There is a history of diarrhoea alternating with constipation during this period. Over the last 2 weeks there has been a history of projectile bilious vomiting associated with a bout of colic. On examination, the patient is pale and dehydrated, and the abdomen has central fullness with visible bowel loops. The bowel sounds are markedly diminished and there is no clinically evident free fluid in the abdomen. An erect X-ray of the abdomen reveals the presence of central abdominal air fluid levels.

cards.text: Gastric outlet obstruction – chronic duodenal ulcer

cards: Mechanism of immunosuppressive agents\nPrevents lymphocyte proliferation
Suxamethonium (succinylcholine)\nWhat is the most frequent complaint made by patients in whom suxamethonium (succinylcholine) has been used?

cards.text: Prolonged action in those with pseudocholinesterase deficiency
Pain at the site of injection

cards: Interventions/assessment in the high-risk surgical patient\nThis refers to treatment aimed at achieving predefined levels of oxygen delivery to tissues. This improves cardiac output, renal output, complication rates and patient survival.
Types of research study\nSeries of patients with a particular disease or condition are compared with matched control patients.
Diagnosis in arthritis\nA patient who had a hip replacement 5 days ago suffers a minor fall. Forty-eight hours later the hip replacement starts to hurt and they feel generally unwell.
Incompletely descended testis\nIn incompletely descended testis, which of the following statements is false?

cards.text: Orchidopexy reduces the chances of developing a testicular tumour.
An incompletely descended testis tends to atrophy as puberty approaches.
A testis absent from the scrotum after 3 months is unlikely to descend.

cards: Types of research study\nTwo randomly allocated treatments are compared.
Thyroid cancers\nA 12-year-old boy presents with a nodule and regional lymph node enlargement.
Ankle injury\nAn elderly woman notices a sudden collapse of the longitudinal arch of her foot with her heel falling into valgus.

cards.text: Surgical repair of the tendon sheath

cards: Management of biliary problems\nA 46-year-old male has been having episodes of colicky RUQ pain for the past 8 months. The pain radiates to his back and is associated with dyspeptic symptoms. Ultrasound scan confirms gall bladder stones and dilated bile ducts with stones. Blood tests show abnormal liver functions. Attempts at ERCP to remove the bile duct stones are unsuccessful.

cards.text: Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) and antegrade stenting
ERCP – stone extraction followed by cholecystectomy

cards: Methods of holding fractures\nA child is involved in a road traffic accident and sustains a fracture across the midshaft of the femur.

Types of allograft reactions\nThis is seen after liver and small-bowel transplants. It frequently involves the skin, causing a characteristic rash on palms and soles. This is serious and it can be fatal.

Postoperative pyrexia\nA 50-year-old patient underwent closure of a perforated duodenal ulcer laparoscopically 5 days ago. He is unwell with a fever of 40oC, with rigors and pain in his right shoulder tip. He is hypoxic. There is oedema of the skin in the right upper quadrant and no air entry in the right lung base.
Shoulder injury\nAn elderly woman running for church trips and lands on her shoulder. The shoulder is swollen and painful with a graze over the deltoid. All movements are restricted.
Investigation and management of the acute airway case\nA stertorous 16-year-old with tonsillitis and a positive test for glandular fever fails to improve with antibiotics, fluids and analgesia.

Vomiting\nMary-Ann, a 10-month-old girl, has been brought in with occasional vomiting for a couple of days. The parents noticed that the vomitus is sometimes green and at other times brownish. They feel that Mary-Ann is in pain intermittently because she screams, flexing her knees and elbows, denoting spasms. They noticed that the nappy has bloodstained mucus. On examination she looks ill and dehydrated and the right side of the abdomen feels empty.

Complications of blood transfusion\nA 34-year-old motorcyclist sustains multiple injuries after an RTA. He is brought to the hospital in severe shock and requires multiple blood transfusions. It is observed that the bleeding is still uncontrolled and the blood fails to clot.

cards.text: Haemolytic transfusion reaction due to incompatibility

cards: Varicose veins\nA 75-year-old man presents with a history of DVT 5 years ago and a recently healed venous ulcer. Duplex scan demonstrates isolated popliteal venous reflux.

cards.text: Saphenofemoral ligation, stripping and stab avulsions

cards: A patient is admitted unconscious with a head injury following a 4-metre fall from the roof of a building. His blood pressure is 80/60 mmHg, pulse 45/min, and he has well-perfused extremities.\nWhat is the safest and most reliable way to clear the cervical spine?

cards.text: Cervical spine series of plain X-rays
Keep him on a spine board until he recovers consciousness.

cards: Carcinoid tumours\nLiver metastasis of tumours that arise from the enterochromaffin cells

Differential diagnosis of acute appendicitis\nA 35-year-old female complains of colicky pain in the right iliac fossa for the last 6 h. In between the attacks she is left with a dull ache. She has vomited a few times and feels feverish. In the past she has had diarrhoea on and off for almost a year. On examination she has a temperature of 38°C, is in discomfort from her pain and has tenderness, rigidity and minimal rebound tenderness in the right iliac fossa.

Shoulder injury\nA patient who sustained a severe intra-articular fracture of the right shoulder as a young man now presents with increasing pain stiffness and weakness of the shoulder. For each scenario, choose the most appropriate treatment:

cards.text: Advice and commiseration; no active treatment

cards: Management of biliary problems\nA 60-year-old male presents with progressive painless jaundice. Examination reveals a palpable non-tender gall bladder. Ultrasound and CT scans show a dilated bile duct with a pancreatic head tumour which is not resectable.

cards.text: Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiography (PTC) and antegrade stenting
ERCP – stone extraction followed by cholecystectomy

cards: Liver infections\nA 75-year-old male diabetic patient complains of anorexia, fever, malaise and right upper quadrant discomfort. On examination there is weight loss and tender hepatomegaly. US shows a multiloculated cystic mass, a finding confirmed on CT scan.
Dysphagia\nA 35-year-old female complains of occasional dysphagia with food sticking in the lower retrosternal region. She has had occasional heartburn for many years.

Premalignant and malignant skin lesions\nThis is a slow-growing, locally invasive tumour (hence also called a rodent ulcer, as it behaves like a rodent burrowing into neighbouring tissues) of a few years’ duration. The edges are typically raised and rolled and occur more often on the face, head and neck.
Renal pathology imaging
Investigation of the pharynx, larynx and neck\nA 46-year-old presents with a pulsatile mass in the left neck which moves side to side but not up and down.

cards.text: CT scan of the neck and chest

cards: Case study – low back pain\nA young rower complains of sudden onset of low back pain when weight- lifting. There is no radiation of the pain and there is no neurological deficit. There is considerable lordosis of the lumbar spine with a step palpable at L5/S1. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Anatomy and pathology\nWhat does the term ‘skip metastasis’ refer to?

cards.text: The tendency of oral cancers to metastasise to lower-echelon cervical lymph nodes without involving the higher echelons.
The tendency of oral cancers to metastasise to the higher-echelon cervical lymph nodes then on to the lower level nodes.
The tendency of oral cancers to metastasise to bone without cervical nodal metastasis.
The tendency of oral cancers to metastasise the contralateral cervical nodes.
The tendency of oral cancers to metastasise to the liver without cervical nodal metastasis.

cards: Clinical presentations of small and large intestine pathology\nA 55-year-old male has presented as an emergency with an acute perianal abscess. He has had frequent loose motions occasionally mixed with blood on and off for many months. He also suffers from colicky abdominal pain. He has a feeling of heaviness in his right iliac fossa. On examination he has an acute perianal abscess and a mass in the right iliac fossa.

Case study – sudden onset of urinary incontinence\nA 30-year-old nurse lifting in the ward experiences sudden pain in her back. She goes to Sister’s office to sit down. The pain eases but now she notices that she has difficulty standing and walking (her legs feel weak and wobbly, and she has been incontinent of urine). What action needs to be taken?

cards.text: Immediate referral to a spine surgeon.

cards: Management of wounds and related conditions\nMultiple debridements followed by definitive closure/repair.
Imaging in trauma\nIn trauma imaging, which of the following statements are false?

cards.text: Focused assessment with sonography for trauma (FAST ) helps in detecting intraperitoneal fluid and cardiac tamponade
CT should not be used when a patient is unstable.
US is useful for diagnosing occult pneumothorax.
In a multiply injured patient, CT of head and spine should be the first line of imaging.
CT is the main imaging method for intracranial, intra-abdominal and vertebral injuries.

cards: Maxillofacial trauma\nA 30-year-old female suffers a fracture of the left nasorbital region. Radiographs and CT scan demonstrate significant disruption of the medial and inferior orbital rims, frontal process of the maxilla and nasal bones.

cards.text: Lower motor neuron VII nerve palsy

cards: Investigations in lymphatic disorders\nThis provides the best reflection of lymphatic function.
Ulcers\nA 60-year-old man presents with a history of pins and needles in his left foot following multiple back operations and an ulcer over the plantar aspect of his second metatarsal head.
Management of wounds and related conditions\nSurgical excision with tissue biopsies sent for culture. Skin graft may be needed later on.
Prophylactic antibiotics regimen\nBroad-spectrum cephalosporin (with anti-staphylococcal action) or gentamicin beads
Anatomy, physiology and pathology of the salivary glands\nThe term plunging ranula refers to which clinical entity?

cards.text: A mucous retention cyst originating from the sublingual glands, limited by the mylohyoid muscle
A mucous retention cyst originating from the submandibular and sublingual glands which perforates the mylohyoid muscle to enter the neck
A benign salivary mass involving the parotid and submandibular glands
A midline neck mass which moves on tongue protrusion.
A malignant congenital salivary mass arising from the submandibular gland

cards: Ankle injury\nA middle-aged active patient develops unilateral heel pain without any trauma. It is worst after sitting still for any length of time.

cards.text: Surgical repair of the tendon sheath

cards: Diagnosis in foot problems\nA middle-aged woman whose work involves being smartly dressed presents with a painful red lump over the medial side of the big toe metatarsophalangeal joint. She is very distressed by the unsightliness of this bump and the fact that she can no longer wear high-heeled shoes comfortably for more than a few minutes.

cards.text: Removal of the prominent bone (osteophyte) and fusion of the joint
Soft-tissue surgery to release tight structures and transfer tendons
Reassurance that this is not abnormal and no treatment is needed or appropriate
An osteotomy to narrow the forefoot, straighten the toe and remove the prominent lump
A careful review by physiotherapy to assess the whole lower limb, then release of tight structures, if appropriate

cards: Management of wounds and related conditions\nExamination under anaesthesia (EUA) with radical excision of all non-bleeding skin. Serial excision is usually done until punctuate dermal bleeding is seen. This is followed by split skin graft.
Treatment of abdominal wall hernias\nDivarication of recti
Aetiology of hip problems\nGenetic predisposition or femoro-acetabular impingement

Bacteria involved in surgical infection\nGram-negative bacillus, which tends to colonise burns and tracheostomy wounds. These can also case UTI. Hospital strains can acquire resistance transferred through plasmids.
Drugs\nWhich of the following drugs have been shown to improve claudication?
Aetiology of anorectal disorders\nThis is evidence of straining during defaecation with an internal intussusception in most cases.

Premalignant and malignant skin lesions\nThere is a pigmented skin lesion on the scalp that has recently changed in colour and has become itchy and started to bleed. There are a few small black spots irregularly scattered around the lesion.

Shoulder injury\nAn elderly patient presents with a painless but weak shoulder. It has got worse over the years. He can only abduct it with a trick movement hunching his shoulder. Otherwise there seems to be very little power of abduction. For each scenario, choose the most appropriate treatment:

cards.text: Advice and commiseration; no active treatment

cards: Diagnosis in foot problems\nA middle-aged runner complains of increasing pain in his right big toe which he broke many years ago. The first metatarsophalangeal joint is red dorsally and has a very limited range of painful movement. The patient wants to be able to continue running.

cards.text: Removal of the prominent bone (osteophyte) and fusion of the joint
A careful review by physiotherapy to assess the whole lower limb, then release of tight structures, if appropriate
An osteotomy to narrow the forefoot, straighten the toe and remove the prominent lump
Soft-tissue surgery to release tight structures and transfer tendons
Reassurance that this is not abnormal and no treatment is needed or appropriate

cards: Anatomy and pathology\nThe term ‘field change’ in relation to oral cancer refers to what?

cards.text: The effect upon the surrounding oral mucosa conferred by long-standing dental caries.
The widespread damage of epithelium leading to mucosal changes and a high incidence of separate tumours.
The wide field radiotherapy techniques which must be applied to the oral mucosa in head and neck cancer patients.
The demarcated mucosal changes seen in patients with oral cancer which resembles the appearance of agricultural fields.
The requirement to rotate the radiotherapy field during a course of treatment to maximise tissue-sparing effects.

cards: Treatment of abdominal wall hernias\nRecurrent inguinal hernia – post-open repair
Fractures, dislocations and subluxations\nA patient is found to have a Power ratio of more than one. What fracture/dislocation/ subluxation is this?
Treatment of rheumatoid arthritis\nA patient with rheumatoid arthritis presents with a painful and very stiff ankle.
Types of allograft reactions\nThis is T-cell-dependent and is characterised by mononuclear cell infiltration. It is reversible.

cards.text: Prescribe analgesia with systemic antibiotics if symptoms do not settle within 48 h

cards: Case study – pain which wakes at night\nA 65-year-old man who has lived all his life in Britain presents with severe and constant back pain at L3, which does not radiate down the legs. It wakes him at night and he has night sweats. X-rays show destruction of the disc space at L2/L3 with invasion of the destruction into the adjacent vertebral bodies. What would you expect to find on needle biopsy?

Treatment options in cancer\nA 56-year-old fit male who underwent surgery for colonic carcinoma 3 years ago is observed to have raised carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) levels. CT scan shows a small solitary left- lobe metastasis. Positron emission tomography (PET) scan does not show any evidence of extrahepatic spread.
The submandibular glands\nWhat percentage of submandibular tumours are malignant?

Head injury\nA 45-year-old man is involved in a road traffic accident. He is deeply unconscious at the scene. Examination shows him to be GCS 7 with a fixed dilated right pupil. He is intubated for transfer to A&E.
Sedation in endoscopy\nRegarding sedation during endoscopy, which of the following statements are false?

cards.text: All sedated patients require pulse oximetry to monitor oxygen saturations.
Co-administration of opiates and benzodiazepines has a synergistic effect.
The use of supplementary oxygen is essential in all sedated patients.
Sedation has no significant dangers and can be used without restrictions.
All sedated patients require secure intravenous access.

cards: Treatment options in cancer\nA 46-year-old female with known ovarian carcinoma presents with features of intestinal obstruction. CT suggests a localised mass which is causing the obstruction. She is otherwise fit and keen on any treatment for her bowel problem.
Lymphoedema\nWhich of the following statements regarding lymphoedema are true?

cards.text: Primary lymphoedema occurs in more than 5 per cent or the population.
Fluid is relatively low in protein in lymphoedema
Lymphoedema often involves the muscle compartments.

cards: Abdominal distension\nA 45-year-old owner of a dog-breeding farm complains of a gradual abdominal distension over the last 6 months that has become painful of late. He is otherwise healthy and has no bowel complaints. Physical examination reveals firm distension of the abdomen which is somewhat tender on palpation and dull on percussion. There is no other significant finding in the abdominal examination. A blood count reveals a TLC of 8600 with a DLC showing eosinophils constituting 12 per cent. An abdominal ultrasound (US) shows the presence of multiple fluid- filled structures spread all over the abdominal viscera.
The immediate postoperative period\nWhich of the following statements are false?

cards.text: Abdominal surgical wounds may compromise postoperative respiratory function.
Infusion and certain monitoring systems can cause complications.
Postoperative deep vein thrombosis (DVT) is classically diagnosed by Homan’s sign.
Oliguria is defined as urinary output of less than 0.5 mL/kg per h.
The commonest cause of postoperative hypotension is bleeding or insufficient fluid administration.

cards: Causes of red eye\nA 63-year-old female presents with a bright red discoloration of the temporal part of the sclera of the right eye. Her vision is normal and she has no discomfort.

Acute abdomen\nA 30-year-old female patient presents with a history of off-and-on colicky central abdominal pain for the past 10 months, associated with weight loss and amenorrhoea over the last 3 months. There is a history of diarrhoea alternating with constipation during this period. In the last week the pain has been continuous and is increasing in severity progressively. There is also a history of absolute constipation over the last 2 days. Her husband suffered from pulmonary tuberculosis in the recent past. On examination, the patient is pale and dehydrated, and the abdomen is distended with the absence of bowel sounds. There is rigidity all over the abdomen with the presence of free fluid. An erect X-ray of the abdomen reveals free gas under the diaphragm.

Different types of operations and infection rates\nOpen viscus surgery or gross spillage or inflammatory bowel disease

Phases of wound healing\nThis phase consists mainly of fibroblast activity and collagen production. The collagen produced during this phase is type 3.

Treatment of anorectal disorders\nAnal polyp
